data.title: Victoria's Secret: Bull Case Is Playing Out Nicely
data.description:
Investment action

I upgraded to a buy rating for Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO) previously, as it has continued to take share in the core intimates business, PINK is working out nicely, and international initiatives are scaling faster than I thought. Since early this year, things have progressed well. The core intimates business is still taking share, the customer file is improving, and international is scaling through a model that should allow growth to continue. I reiterate a buy rating.

The core business is showing proof that the turnaround is real

My previous thesis was anchored on the idea that the core intimates business was stabilizing, but fast forward to today, I think there is proof that it is doing much better, and more importantly, it is broader. The pushback previously was that any form of improvement was due to a few product wins or easier y/y comps, and I think the recent numbers we have should put that debate to rest.

First of all, VSCO continued to win total intimates market share for the third consecutive quarter, with share up low single digits. This is a strong signal that customers are choosing VSCO more often. Note that this is a mature category, and winning market share is not easy unless product relevance is actually improving. Secondly, the Victoria's Secret bra business grew mid-single digits in the latest Q4 and had finally seen annual growth for the first time since 2021. This second point is a huge one because, for VSCO, bras are the key growth driver for this business. You can think of VSCO's bras segment as the main funnel for its other assortment. When bras are working, the traffic and halo effect spreads into other categories. For instance, if a consumer goes into a VSCO store because of an attractive-looking bra, chances are they may be attracted to a similarly designed/concepted panty. That is exactly what happened here. Demand for panties accelerated and delivered their best performance since 2021.

The customer base is improving

This is another important part that makes the bull case more exciting. Management said overall customer count grew at a low single-digit rate, driven primarily by new customer acquisition, including younger customers. Retention among existing customers also improved, and spend per customer went up mid-single digits. These datapoints tell us a lot of important things at once.

  1. VSCO is capturing fresh demand (new customers). This is important because a turnaround does not last very long if growth comes only from the same customer base.
  2. The existing customer base is not churning at the same pace. This makes the recovery look more sustainable and also current product assortment is not alienating the core base.
  3. Existing customers are spending more. This suggests better product relevance and should translate to higher margins.
  4. VSCO managed to address one of the most important bearish debates, that it may lose brand relevance over time (an aging customer base). Clearly, that is not true as younger customers are joining the customer base.

If we look at the app data, it adds another bullish layer on this point. App downloads have increased 25% in the latest quarter, and apps now drive about one-third of digital sales. This is a sizeable mix of the business and shows that VSCO is creating a more direct customer relationship, which paves the way for VSCO to communicate new products faster, personalize offers better, and keep customers more engaged with the brand.

International momentum

The momentum outside of North America shouldn't be overlooked either. As a recap, in Q4 2025, international sales grew 43% (significantly faster than consolidated). While that figure benefited from the shift of European Union digital sales into the international channel, even if we adjusted for that, international still grew by 27%. I think one important component of the growth equation here is the way it is structured. Internationally, the setup includes franchise stores and digital channels, wholesale travel retail, joint ventures in China and the UK, and also a company-owned digital site that fulfills orders outside the US.

There are some operational risks with this type of model (lesser control, for example), but the reward is the pace of expansion can be much faster (operationally). Operationally, VSCO does not need to build every market by itself from day one. The local partners can help to open stores, operate digital channels, and, most importantly, help to execute locally. The last point is incredibly important because of different cultures, trends, and consumer preferences. This should allow VSCO to expand more aggressively into more markets at the same time.

In other words, I think this setup gives VSCO more ways to grow: either through a partner-led store expansion, through wholesale and royalty streams, through joint ventures, or even through its own digital channel where it sees direct demand. Longer-term, this international segment should gradually help to balance out the heavy dependence on the North American market, which should justify a higher valuation multiple.

Valuation

For a business that is executing extremely well and beating latest consensus estimates in its Q4 earnings, the sharp contraction in valuation multiples is not warranted in my view. VSCO now trades at ~12.5x forward PE vs. ~21x previously. Forward earnings growth expectations are actually better than during my previous update, with consensus now expecting mid-to-high-teen adjusted EPS growth through FY28 (Bloomberg's FY29 is actual FY28). The average multiple of peers is ~18.5x with an earnings growth outlook of ~10% for the next two years. Comparing it this way, VSCO deserves to trade at a premium, and I think ~18x is probably the right multiple since this is where Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT) trades at with high-teens earnings growth. Just from multiples re-rating alone, from 12.5x to 18x, that is close to a 50% upside.

Risk

The recent momentum may start to falter if VSCO fails to keep product newness and brand relevance high in core categories like bras and panties. I am positive on the momentum, but if management messes up on execution at this point, the entire turnaround story may come under heavy challenge by the bears. On top of that, as I said, international growth depends partly on franchise, license, wholesale, and joint venture partners, which means VSCO has less direct control over execution as it scales overseas.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for VSCO, as the bull case is playing out nicely. The core business is gaining share, bras are once again driving demand into adjacent categories, the customer base is getting healthier, and international momentum is strong. The stock is now only trading at ~12.5x forward earnings despite better forward growth expectations and strong execution. My view is that this disconnect is too wide, and if management keeps executing at this level, multiples should move up.

data.title: Henkel Buys Olaplex - Implications Are Negative
data.description:
I could, of course, have provided this article about 3 weeks ago, before the crash. However, because my recent article on Henkel (HENKY) was as little as 2 months ago, I thought it was over-ambitious to cover the company because of a mere surge in valuation. I was of the opinion, and still am, that my coverage at the time took into consideration the potential of not only the surge in valuation, but also the subsequent crash which we have been seeing since. So, since my last piece on the company, Henkel has actually performed poorly - but it would be accurate to at the same time claim that the market has not fared that much better either.

So, what we have here is a significant up and down in a very short time. My first question to you, dear reader, would be if you believe there is any factual or objective ground for this movement, or if it's based more on subjective speculation and expectations - external factors, as they were? I believe the answer to this question (the latter part) should be affirmative.

Nothing has happened in the last 2 months to first make Henkel suddenly worth 20% more in a short time, only to be followed by a significant decline shortly after. While the company's earnings, which were reported not that long ago, were certainly a part of the decline (4% or so) due to an earnings miss (4Q) and low 2026E guidance, as well as a 2025 organic sales miss of 0.7%, I don't think this should be the result.

It's my conviction that Henkel at 12-13x P/E is neither expensive nor in any way unattractively valued. While the surge to almost 15.5x P/E before the market crash was a slight bit of premiumization, it was not outside the realm of historical results here.

In this article, I will do two things. Establish an update for Henkel with changes (if any) for the valuation due to 2026E, and also talk about the Olaplex M&A that was reported about 2-3 hours ago at the time that I am writing this piece.

Henkel - M&A's, results, and 2026E - what it means for the sentiment and valuation

First off, nothing has changed insofar as Henkel's appeal goes, in my view. A slight miss in organic growth, if that was the cause of a significant decline, is an overreaction of a very high order - I would classify that under "opportunity", and Henkel trading at below €70/share is this (which I will be showing you going forward).

But before we get into 4Q and 2026, let's look at the Olaplex M&A. I'm torn in this case. As I've talked about in my previous articles, I like the company's industrial adhesives more than I do the consumer portfolio. Olaplex is hair care - I like Stahl more than I do this one. Olaplex, for me, on the basis of an admittedly cursory review, seems to be a non-starter for several reasons.

First off, Olaplex (OLPX) has been embroiled in a class-action lawsuit claiming its products cause a variety of issues. While these have largely been dismissed, this has impacted consumer reputation, and I argue this has eroded the company's valuation.

On the face of it, the valuation may seem "cheap".

But given they pay $1.4B, I wouldn't necessarily call it so. This isn't just because of the lawsuits, but also due to ingredients (which the company have then recalled).

What you see above is what I consider to be a failed IPO, and a failed valuation case for what amounts to a struggling business. Ergo, Henkel is picking up a struggling business, which, based on everything I can see is also struggling with declining operating margins. The 55% premium that the company has decided to pay over the closing price is, to me, another argument against this 'marriage', if you will. If you were to come to me and say that you're thinking of combining a legacy company in Germany with execution risks and challenges in its consumer division (all of which Henkel has), with a US-based challenged and fraught IPO with a business gone non-profitable, (which Olaplex is, see below), I would probably react with raised eyebrows.

The notion here is to strengthen the hair care portfolio. I do not believe this to be a good move, not with this target in mind. While it could be argued that the company or brand could get back to its sales numbers, I believe this vastly oversimplifies the entire consumer hair care sector. It also entirely misses the point that the premium the company had was due to high-growth appreciation for its brand - which I believe to be unrecoverable. While I have no doubt that Olaplex can be profitable, I do not see what differentiates it from alternatives that Henkel either could have bought, or even developed at a cheaper cost. The notion that the company's GM (Gross margins) is something that Henkel can "fix", again misses the point that there's no more exclusivity in this specific sector (Olaplex was one of the brands that worked the whole "bond-building" category in haircare, which allowed for premiums now, in my view, no longer there).

It becomes, as it is for Henkel, about the cost of customer acquisition as well as the cost of retention - neither of which is appealing for a company in this state.

I therefore view the move as not a positive one - but time will tell whether I am overreacting here. (I obviously might be).

So, 4Q25.

The unfortunate fact remains that I consider consumer brands to be the risk rather than the upside. I discount for them - I do not premiumize for them. The adhesives segment is the bread and butter and what I view as growth potential, because of the way it is used and what end markets require the products.

Specifically, I want to draw your attention to the fact that automotive, industrials, and electronics all use the "stuff" that Henkel makes - it's an industry standard setter with a global 14% market share. This is where its strength lies, and where I believe it should focus its attention going forward.

But I do not personally believe that 2025 was a bad year. With a solid sales performance, organic growth of almost 1%, and an EBIT of €3B, Henkel continues to be a cash printer. The trick is to treat it like a long-term mature "cash cow", rather than a growth company. It is not, in my view, a growth company. It's a cash printer that, even on a conservative basis, generates nearly €2B of annual FCF, of which we get a yield of 3%. It's very decent. And with reversals and valuation high, we can easily get to 15% annualized.

That's how I work with this business. Operationally, there was no negative 2025 issues either - quite the contrary. Henkel continued to merge its operations, even completing the consumer segment ahead of time.

The problem becomes when the company tries to go beyond where it should, as I believe they are doing now. I believe the Stahl M&A was good. It's a it's a strong move, by virtue of its complementary strategic coatings move. It adds €1B to sales, and a high-margin industrial focus - exactly what we'd like to see. We can also see the positive market reaction to this - it was a major contributor to the upside here. I like it, my colleagues liked it, I haven't actually talked to anyone who considered this a "bad move".

Olaplex is very different - and I will be marking the company down for it.

Valuation for Henkel - Lowering my PT due to Olaplex

What I'm going to do is add the $1.4B to the balance sheet as debt, which not only reduces capital availability for other M&A's, but also either reduces cash or increases interest expenses. If Olaplex fails to provide EPS growth, this will be dilutive for us Henkel shareholders - and that is what I will be assuming here, as well as my "base" scenario.

I therefore take a decidedly bearish view of this move - but always happy to be proven wrong here.

Henkel's balance sheet is ironclad. $1.4B won't disturb the A-rating. The company has less than €600M in net debt, so even adding all of that as debt (and ignoring the cash and equivalents of about €2.7B at the end of FY25, enough to pay for this M&A, though recent figures suggest that after M&A's, it's at about €110M). Scope has already confirmed that such an addition is credit neutral.

Henkel has a credit threshold of 2x. It's currently below 1x, and even with the addition, it would remain at or below 1x. It's, in fact, payable by the company's annualized free cash flow for a single year, and the company would have almost 45% of that FCF remaining, if looking at 2025 for guidance. Even an interest cost increase of €70M, based on the current average interest, would do very little to impact the interest coverage ratio that is over 30x.

As such, despite my admitted vitriol above, my adjustment will have to be conservative to remain fair. But I would not, at the same time, allow Henkel to assume any profit from this M&A until it actually becomes clear that Olaplex can provide net income, which, as it currently stands, it cannot.

The small size of the impact means that my adjustment will be made on a discretionary basis. My previous PT was €77/share, which means buying at a P/E of about 13.4x. I'm adding €70M in interest expense to the company's income statement, which comes at current SO to €0.13/share, which means the forecast for 2026 has just become "flat", rather than a slight growth. That means that €77/share is no longer a good PT. I'm lowering it to €73/share to account for this, and maintain at 13.4x.

I am, for the time being, choosing not toimpact Henkel for brand impairment and legal costs, which could become part of Henkel's story after Olaplex. But I've seen some assume that the worst-case scenario here is if the brand is simply a "zero add". That is not the worst-case scenario. This instead includes legal liabilities and other issues.

For now, I'm comfortable remaining here. Henkel is still a "BUY", but one with somewhat lower appeal because of this move.

Risks & Considerations

The main risk for Henkel is related to its consumer segments, which I believe are sub-par in terms of market-beating upside even after the divestments - that's why I focus so much on the industrial side of things. While the company has leadership in key segments, I don't see how the company would be able to drive profit with just this (confirmed by more than 60% of operating profit coming from adhesives, but only 50% of revenues). So, in forecasting Henkel, we need to discount that, and I have - if the company was a pure-play industrial, the share price would be put at 10-20% higher in terms of targets. I maintain this view in March of 2026, after Olaplex.

Furthermore, as the "icing" on the cake here in terms of risk, look at COGS exposure. Much of the company's costs are made efficient and improved, but the company can't "efficiency away" input costs and commodity risks. Raw material price cycles are another central risk to the business.

Thesis

Henkel is one of the more qualitative players in this entire segment. Peers in adhesive peers offer alternatives, but the margins speak for themselves, as does the company's market share, 3x the share of the closest competitor. However, the consumer brands are the risk-carriers - and the M&A of Olaplex adds to this, in my view.

Henkel seems to have, as of 3Q25, 4Q25, and going into 2026, righted some of its underperforming segments and operations. Stahl was also an excellent M&A, unlike the latest one the company has taken on.

With its refreshed focus, including a higher upside from Adhesives due to things like AI pushes, I believe the company is in a prime position for an undervalued investment. I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here in 2026, despite Olaplex. My price target is €74/share, updated for 2026. For the HEN3 ADR, which is HENOY, the PT comes to ~$21.5.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €74/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Logical Skepticism For Peace Pulls The Petrodollar Higher - EUR/USD, AUD/USD And Dollar Index Overview
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US dollar (DXY) has been in the spotlight over the past two months, after remaining the pet peeve for FX since early 2025.

With wartime, however, things can change fast and decisively: the global reserve has risen by 4.40% since its end-January lows in a blink.

The new Fed Chair elect, hawkish repricings, Petrodollars, and a general backing away from risky trades built up sudden demand, particularly at a time when asset managers were the most bearish on the USD in 14 years. And when the market is stuck on one side of a trade, it often results in huge reversals.

The Petrodollar trade, however, was the fuel for the currency market throughout this month, and despite what seemed like a relative dissonance this week, as the tone sours again, the correlation is coming right back.

Indeed, after cautious optimism throughout this week's trading following the announcement of the US-Iran talks, it seems that Iran is still heavily inclined to maintain its strategic advantage over the Strait of Hormuz and its ballistic missile capabilities - two of the most contentious points in the indirect talks.

But the real issue for markets is that President Trump's tone has gradually grown more pessimistic, and this coincides with the fact that the huge Marine fleet is arriving in the Arabian Sea in less than a day. So where are these mere distraction tactics?

Good question, as war strategy is something that us common mortals cannot fully grasp. The Art of War, legendary treatise, can help in that sort (and is also a great read for traders).

Deception tactics are common, and nobody can really understand what any side has in mind.

What is sure, however, is that WTI is now rebounding toward $95, the higher bound of its momentum pivot, and above the psychological level, sentiment will sour even further.

At least, the US dollar is enjoying this move, amid a more hesitant FX session. As further clarity is expected in the next 24 hours, consider current levels as indications of doubtful neutrality amid the ongoing war.

Anything above in oil and the dollar implies a worsening in conditions and sentiment, which should drag into next week (+ Pessimistic)

On the other hand, a return below $90 in WTI will be most welcome to investors (+ Optimistic)

We will look at the Dollar Index, EUR/USD, and AUD/USD to assess the current state of the market and whether more upside is warranted for the dollar amid resurfacing doubts.

Dollar Index 4H Chart

The US dollar attempted a corrective sequence as the US-Iran talks were announced, but with WTI not correcting much further, the currency still receives fundamental support.

A few technical elements are developing in the Dollar Index which can help to find trades in major FX pairs:

  • A short-term, relatively flat bear channel has developed and contained the index movement. Traders may either use it as points of entry to long and short the US dollar or as a breakout signal. We are currently reaching its highs (99.90).
    • Its lows are at around 98.65.
  • A more neutral 99.00-100.00 range is holding for now. It is less responsive but more stable to watch the higher time frame movement.

Levels of interest for the Dollar Index:

Resistance Levels

  • Morning spike 99.93 and channel top
  • Weekly range highs 100.00
  • 100.00-100.50 main resistance zone
  • War highs 100.544

Support Levels

  • Intraday micro support 99.30
  • 98.70-99.00 support (mini range lows)
  • 98.00 2025 support
  • Support 97.40-97.60
  • 2025 lows 96.40-96.80 support

AUD/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

AUD/USD just broke its past month's 0.6970-0.7150 major range to the downside, indicating high potential for a further correction.

Fundamentals are changing for the Aussie dollar after a strong period after two consecutive hikes from the RBA, particularly after Australian CPI surprised to the downside.

Failing to rebound above 0.6980 would hint at more bearish activity in the major pair.

Levels of interest for AUD/USD:

Resistance Levels

  • Dec 2021 lows 0.6970-0.70 major pivot (broken range holds above)
  • Mini resistance 4H MA 50 – 0.7020; bullish above
  • 2023 highs from 0.71-0.7150 resistance
  • 0.71867 March highs
  • June 2022 extremes 0.72-0.7230

Support Levels

  • 0.69-0.6935 early Feb support
  • 0.69015 session lows
  • Micro support 0.6850 (+/- 30 pips)
  • October 2024 mini-support 0.6750 (+/- 100 pips)

EUR/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD is now turning bearish, breaking its 4H 50-period MA after a double top this week and crossing below its pivot zone (1.1540-1.1570).

This hints at a test around at least the 1.1475-1.15 November support, which could extend further to the 1.1410 war lows if the situation deteriorates.

Levels to place on your EUR/USD charts:

Resistance Levels

  • Immediate resistance 1.1546
  • Resistance 1.16250-1.16350
  • 1.1650-1.17 March Resistance
  • 1.1750 mini-resistance and channel top
  • Resistance zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)

Support Levels

  • 1.1475-1.15 November support
  • War lows 1.1410
  • 1.1350-1.14 support
  • 1.12-1.13 next main support zone

Safe trades, and keep a close eye on Middle East developments!

Original Post
